mappings.rationale: The definitive introduction, spanning A (history of agriculture), B (farm management), C (crop farming), and D (livestock farming) in a single volume.
Addresses the downstream side of agriculture: food processing (F), preservation (G), quality control (H), and the search for new food sources (I).
Evaluating food quality (H.1) and developing new food products (I) requires the nutritional science framework this volume provides.
Plant biology underpins crop farming—propagation, seeding, and cultivation (C.2)—and the major cereal and horticultural crops in C.6.
Pest and disease control (C.5) and pollution management (B.4) depend on ecological principles that govern agricultural ecosystems.
Forestry is a branch of crop farming, and this volume addresses forest crops including timber and rubber (C.6.c).
Soil microbial communities drive nutrient cycling essential to C.1 (soil preparation) and influence C.5 (pest and disease control) in crop production.
Fermentation drives beverage production (F.8, brewing and wine) and food preservation (G.1.d, pickling), while microbiological methods assess food quality (H.1).
Fungi play dual roles in agriculture: as crop disease organisms (C.5) and as essential agents in yeast-based fermentation for brewing, baking (F.8), and preservation (G.1.d).
Insects are both the primary pest threat to crops (C.5) and, as pollinators and honey producers, valuable livestock in their own right (D.2.f, bees).
Genetic principles govern both animal breeding (D.1) and plant propagation (C.2), making this essential for understanding crop and livestock improvement.
Selective breeding and hereditary principles have shaped livestock improvement (D.1) for millennia, from Bakewell's sheep to modern genomics.
The biology of cattle, swine, sheep, goats, and horses provides the zoological context for D.2 (major livestock animals).
Fishing technology (E) and marine products processing (F.6) depend on understanding marine ecosystems and the species they sustain.
Bacteria are agents of fermentation and pickling (G.1.d), threats to livestock health (D.3), and subjects of microbiological food quality evaluation (H.1).
Livestock disease and pest control (D.3) and food safety (H) both require understanding how infectious agents spread and can be contained.
Shifting weather patterns reshape farming (B.4) and threaten specialized crop production (C.4), making climate science essential to agricultural planning.
Sustainable agriculture balances pollution control (B.4) with the need to develop new food sources (I), a challenge this volume addresses directly.
Invasive pests and disease organisms (C.5) threaten crop farming worldwide, and this volume explains their pathways, impacts, and management.
